Every operator in our Montevideo network holds Ministry of Interior licensing within one of South America’s most developed regulatory frameworks, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration, standard company licence verification practice, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Paramaribo
item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Paramaribo: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Paramaribo network holds Ministry of Justice and Police authorisation, and is assessed for local competence within a less developed regulatory framework. It covers standard team configuration, extra operator vetting, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Port-au-Prince
item.description:
There is no functioning national private security regulatory authority operating consistently in Port-au-Prince as of 2026. This page covers who can realistically support a Haiti-linked requirement under these conditions: operators must be selected through international security firms with verified Haiti experience, given the collapse of any reliable licensing body, and it covers the extreme due-diligence standard applied given the security vacuum and ongoing gang conflict.

Port-au-Prince CPO: the framework for essential visits

The FCDO advises against all travel to Haiti. The US State Department classifies Haiti at Level 4 (Do Not Travel). These advisories reflect the ground reality. CPO engagement in Port-au-Prince is therefore for essential visits only: UN operations, senior NGO leadership, diplomatic principal movement, and assessed essential business visits. The CPO team is structured as a professional security operation, not a driver-plus-escort arrangement. Source: FCDO Haiti travel advice (2026); US State Dept Haiti Level 4 advisory (2026).

item.title: Close Protection Officers in San Salvador
item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in San Salvador: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our San Salvador network is registered under the Ministerio de Justicia y Seguridad Publica’s Ley de Regulacion de Servicios de Seguridad, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration, specific armed-guard licensing, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Tegucigalpa
item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Tegucigalpa: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Tegucigalpa network holds authorisation from the Secretaria de Seguridad under the Ley de Seguridad Privada, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration, Interior Ministry authorisation requirements, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Ulaanbaatar
item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Ulaanbaatar: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Ulaanbaatar network is registered with the State Registration Authority under the Law on Private Security Activities, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration, weather-contingency planning, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Vientiane
item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Vientiane: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Vientiane network is registered with the Ministry of Public Security, and is assessed for local competence within a less developed regulatory framework than counterpart Southeast Asian systems. It covers standard team configuration and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Yaounde
item.description:
This page is about the people who provide close protection in Yaounde: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Yaounde network holds MINAT authorisation, with individual guard police registration, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

The Yaounde MINAT licensing framework

Cameroon’s Ministere de l’Administration Territoriale (MINAT) administers private security licensing in Yaounde. Company and individual licensing verification is the required pre-engagement step. Operator due diligence is particularly important given the variability of standards in the Yaounde security sector.

item.title: Djibouti City
item.description: Djibouti City is the capital of the Republic of Djibouti, a small nation at the Bab-el-Mandeb strait – the narrow waterway between the Red Sea and the Gulf of Aden through which approximately 10 to 15 percent of global trade passes. This geographic position has made Djibouti one of the world’s most strategically concentrated locations for foreign military basing: the US AFRICOM operates Camp Lemonnier (the only permanent US military base in Africa), France maintains the Forces Francaises stationnees a Djibouti (FFDJ), China operates its only overseas naval logistics facility, Japan maintains a Self-Defence Force base, and several other nations have bilateral security arrangements. The Doraleh Multi-Purpose Port serves as Ethiopia’s primary maritime gateway for landlocked East Africa’s largest economy.

item.title: Event Security in Ashgabat
item.description: The capital of turkmenistan and the administrative centre of one of the world’s largest natural gas producers, with a significant diplomatic community and energy sector corporate presence operating under close state surveillance. Event security in Ashgabat addresses the specific risk categories relevant to Turkmenistan – heavy state surveillance, movement restrictions outside Ashgabat, and the regulatory constraints of operating in a highly controlled authoritarian environment – with licensed operators, pre-event venue assessment, and vetted delegate transport.

item.title: Event Security in Asuncion
item.description:
The capital of paraguay and the financial and administrative centre of a regional economy with significant agricultural export, energy (itaipu hydroelectric), and informal trade sectors. Event security in Asuncion addresses the specific risk categories relevant to Paraguay – petty crime, bag-snatching, and express kidnapping risk in the informal economy districts – with licensed operators, pre-event venue assessment, and vetted delegate transport.

The Asuncion event security environment

Asuncion hosts corporate conferences, diplomatic receptions, and industry sector gatherings for the financial services, agricultural exports, and regional corporate headquarters. The FCDO assesses the Paraguay threat environment at moderate risk. Source: FCDO Paraguay travel advice (2026); US State Department Paraguay advisory Level 2 (2026).

item.title: Event Security in Belize City
item.description: Belize City is the largest city in Belize and the centre of the country’s maritime, fisheries, tourism, and offshore financial services sectors. The Radisson Fort George Hotel and Best Western Biltmore Plaza in the Fort George district are the principal venues for international business events, offering a manageable operating environment within a city that carries elevated gang-related crime in its southern districts. Belize’s English-speaking environment and its legal and financial services sector make it an active venue for offshore financial conferences and investment events.

item.title: Event Security in Bishkek
item.description:
The capital of kyrgyzstan and a significant central asian trade and logistics hub, with an active diplomatic community and growing mining and energy sector corporate presence. Event security in Bishkek addresses the specific risk categories relevant to Kyrgyzstan – petty crime, periodic political unrest, and organised criminal networks with documented involvement in cargo theft – with licensed operators, pre-event venue assessment, and vetted delegate transport.

The Bishkek event security environment

Bishkek hosts corporate conferences, diplomatic receptions, and industry sector gatherings for the regional trade, mining, and diplomatic hub. The FCDO assesses the Kyrgyzstan threat environment at moderate risk. Source: FCDO Kyrgyzstan travel advice (2026); US State Department Kyrgyzstan advisory Level 2 (2026).

item.title: Event Security in Chittagong
item.description:
Bangladesh’s second city and principal port, handling the majority of the country’s garment exports and serving as the operational base for international shipping and textile sector delegations. Event security in Chittagong addresses the specific risk categories relevant to Bangladesh – terrorism, political strikes (hartals), and petty crime targeting port and garment-sector visitors – with licensed operators, pre-event venue assessment, and vetted delegate transport.

The Chittagong event security environment

Chittagong hosts corporate conferences, diplomatic receptions, and industry sector gatherings for the Bangladesh’s main port and garment manufacturing hub. The FCDO assesses the Bangladesh threat environment at high risk. Source: FCDO Bangladesh travel advice (2026); US State Department Bangladesh advisory Level 2 (2026).

item.title: Event Security in Dushanbe
item.description:
The capital of tajikistan and the administrative centre of a country with close borders to afghanistan and active engagement from international development finance organisations and energy sector investors. Event security in Dushanbe addresses the specific risk categories relevant to Tajikistan – border proximity risks, political sensitivity, and petty crime in market areas – with licensed operators, pre-event venue assessment, and vetted delegate transport.

The Dushanbe event security environment

Dushanbe hosts corporate conferences, diplomatic receptions, and industry sector gatherings for the development finance, energy sector, and diplomatic missions. The FCDO assesses the Tajikistan threat environment at moderate risk. Source: FCDO Tajikistan travel advice (2026); US State Department Tajikistan advisory Level 2 (2026).

item.title: Event Security in Georgetown
item.description: Georgetown is the capital of Guyana and, since the discovery and development of the Stabroek offshore oil block, has become one of the fastest-growing business event destinations in the Caribbean and South American region. ExxonMobil, Hess, SBM Offshore, and the international finance institutions now bring major conferences and investment forums to the Marriott and Pegasus hotel venues. Georgetown’s event security environment reflects this rapid transformation: significant wealth and international executive visibility in a city that carries elevated robbery, violent crime, and kidnapping risk.

item.title: Event Security in Guatemala City
item.description: Guatemala City is the capital of Guatemala and the largest city in Central America, hosting the region’s principal commodity sector events, regional trade forums, and diplomatic gatherings. Zone 10 (Zona Viva) and Zone 14 provide the primary event venue and hotel cluster for international visitors, concentrated around the Westin Camino Real, Hilton Guatemala City, and the Centro de Convenciones America. Guatemala City’s event security environment reflects one of the region’s highest rates of kidnapping and violent crime targeting perceived high-value individuals, requiring structured security planning for any organised international event.

item.title: Event Security in Guayaquil
item.description: Ecuador’s largest city and principal commercial centre, handling the majority of ecuador’s trade flows including banana and oil exports, with a significant increase in security incidents linked to drug trafficking networks from 2023 onwards. Event security in Guayaquil addresses the specific risk categories relevant to Ecuador – armed robbery, kidnapping for ransom, carjacking, and gang-related violence linked to drug trafficking networks with significant presence in the port and peripheral neighbourhoods – with licensed operators, pre-event venue assessment, and vetted delegate transport.

item.title: Event Security in Kingston
item.description: Kingston is Jamaica’s capital and principal business city, host to the Caribbean’s financial services, energy, and regional trade event calendar. The Jamaica Pegasus, Knutsford Court, and Jamaica Conference Centre in Kingston Harbour are the primary event venues, all concentrated in the New Kingston and downtown areas. Kingston’s corporate event environment is shaped by a significant ambient crime risk: the city has one of the Caribbean’s highest murder rates and documented robbery targeting of international visitors, making structured event security planning a professional requirement, not an optional add-on.

item.title: Event Security in La Paz
item.description: The seat of government of bolivia and one of the highest capital cities in the world at approximately 3,640m above sea level, housing the bolivian government ministries, the diplomatic community, and the mining sector corporate offices. Event security in La Paz addresses the specific risk categories relevant to Bolivia – street crime, express kidnapping, political demonstrations that can block major routes, and altitude-related medical risk at 3,640m above sea level – with licensed operators, pre-event venue assessment, and vetted delegate transport.

item.title: Event Security in Managua
item.description: Managua is the capital of Nicaragua and the operational base for the country’s agricultural commodity sector, government affairs activity, and international development finance presence. The Hyatt Place, Hotel Intercontinental, and Camino Real venues along the Carretera Masaya and in Altamira provide the primary conference facilities for international events. Managua’s event security environment is shaped by two distinct risk categories: the ambient street crime in less managed city areas, and the political surveillance environment stemming from the Ortega government’s restrictions on civil society and international organisations since the 2018 political crisis.

item.title: Event Security in Montevideo
item.description: The capital of uruguay and consistently rated as one of the safest cities in latin america, with a significant financial services sector, regional corporate hub status, and a well-regulated private security industry. Event security in Montevideo addresses the specific risk categories relevant to Uruguay – petty theft in tourist areas and peripheral neighbourhood crime, both at low levels relative to Latin American regional norms – with licensed operators, pre-event venue assessment, and vetted delegate transport.

item.title: Event Security in Paramaribo
item.description: Paramaribo is the capital of Suriname and the staging base for the country’s gold, bauxite, and oil sectors – each of which draws international executives and investment conferences to the city’s principal hotel venues. The Torarica Hotel and Courtyard by Marriott are the primary venues for international corporate events, situated along the Suriname River in the most manageable zone for international visitors. Suriname’s growing offshore oil sector and active gold mining industry mean that Paramaribo’s event calendar is expanding, with an increasingly international delegate profile requiring structured security planning.

item.title: Event Security in Port-au-Prince
item.description: Port-au-Prince is the capital of Haiti and remains the operational centre for the country’s UN, NGO, and diplomatic community despite the extreme security environment. The Multinational Security Support Mission (MSS) has been present since 2024 with a mandate to support the Haitian National Police, but gang control of significant metropolitan areas remains a defining factor in all event security planning. Petionville – the hillside district above the city – is the primary viable location for international organised events, with the Hotel Montana and Villa Creole as the main suitable venues.

item.title: Event Security in San Salvador
item.description: San Salvador is the capital of El Salvador and has undergone a significant security transformation since the Bukele government’s gang suppression programme beginning in 2022. The dramatic reduction in gang activity has repositioned San Salvador from a high-risk to a medium-risk environment for international business visitors, with the Escalon, Zona Rosa, and Santa Elena districts providing a manageable event hosting environment. International investor activity – driven in part by El Salvador’s Bitcoin legal tender status and its active pursuit of foreign direct investment – has increased the demand for structured event security in San Salvador.

item.title: Event Security in Tegucigalpa
item.description:
Tegucigalpa is the capital of Honduras and the base for the country’s government affairs, development finance, and regional business event activity. The principal event venues in Lomas del Guijarro and Palmira – the most internationally oriented business districts – draw regional executives, diplomatic delegations, and development finance visitors to a city that carries one of Central America’s highest crime and kidnapping risk profiles.

The Tegucigalpa event security environment

The FCDO advises against all but essential travel to significant areas of Honduras, including several municipalities adjacent to Tegucigalpa. The Lomas del Guijarro and Palmira districts are rated as more manageable by the FCDO, but the city as a whole carries elevated violent crime, extortion targeting organisations with international funding, and kidnapping risk. The US State Department classifies Honduras at Level 3 (Reconsider Travel). Event security planning must address the gap between the managed hotel venue environment and the broader city risk level. Source: FCDO Honduras travel advice (2026); US State Dept Honduras Level 3 advisory (2026).

item.title: Event Security in Ulaanbaatar
item.description:
The capital and only major urban centre of mongolia, home to mining sector corporate offices, international development organisations, diplomatic missions, and the country’s entire financial services sector. Event security in Ulaanbaatar addresses the specific risk categories relevant to Mongolia – petty crime including bag-snatching and pickpocketing, drink-spiking in entertainment districts, and extreme winter conditions – with licensed operators, pre-event venue assessment, and vetted delegate transport.

The Ulaanbaatar event security environment

Ulaanbaatar hosts corporate conferences, diplomatic receptions, and industry sector gatherings for the mining, resources, and diplomatic capital. The FCDO assesses the Mongolia threat environment at moderate risk. Source: FCDO Mongolia travel advice (2026); US State Department Mongolia advisory Level 1 (2026).

item.title: Event Security in Vientiane
item.description:
The capital of laos and the administrative, diplomatic, and development finance centre of a one-party state with significant chinese and multilateral investment presence. Event security in Vientiane addresses the specific risk categories relevant to Laos – petty theft, road accidents, and the regulatory constraints of operating in a one-party state – with licensed operators, pre-event venue assessment, and vetted delegate transport.

The Vientiane event security environment

Vientiane hosts corporate conferences, diplomatic receptions, and industry sector gatherings for the diplomatic, development finance, and natural resource sector hub. The FCDO assesses the Laos threat environment at low risk. Source: FCDO Laos travel advice (2026); US State Department Laos advisory Level 1 (2026).

item.title: Event Security in Yaounde
item.description: Yaounde is the political capital of Cameroon and the base for the country’s diplomatic community, government affairs activity, and international development finance operations. The Hilton Yaounde and Hotel Mont Febe in the Bastos diplomatic district, and the Palais des Congres, are the primary venues for international events. Cameroon’s oil sector, its position in the Central African region, and the large AfDB and World Bank country operation all generate a regular international conference calendar in Yaounde. The event security environment reflects Cameroon’s high-risk rating, shaped by robbery and kidnapping risk in the city and the broader Anglophone crisis security context.

item.title: Executive Protection in Ashgabat
item.description: Executive protection in Ashgabat is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies to protect a travelling executive against government surveillance and access and visa restrictions: advance work on venues and routes, a written risk assessment, an operations controller, and approved secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a C-suite visit, where discretion is a leading design factor given the regulatory environment.

item.title: Executive Protection in Asuncion
item.description: Executive protection in Asuncion is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies to protect a travelling executive against drug trafficking, organised crime, and corruption exposure: advance work on venues and routes, a written risk assessment, an operations controller, and Ministry of Interior licensed secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a C-suite visit, where the need is layered, documented risk management.

item.title: Executive Protection in Bishkek
item.description: Executive protection in Bishkek is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies to protect a travelling executive against political instability and organised-crime exposure: advance work on venues and routes, a written risk assessment, an operations controller, and Ministry of Internal Affairs licensed secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a C-suite visit, where the need is layered, documented risk management rather than ad-hoc cover.

item.title: Executive Protection in Chittagong
item.description: Executive protection in Chittagong is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies to protect a travelling executive against terrorism awareness and political instability and strike action: advance work on venues and routes, a written threat assessment, an operations controller, and Ministry of Home Affairs registered secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a C-suite visit, where the need is layered, documented risk management.

item.title: Executive Protection in Dushanbe
item.description: Executive protection in Dushanbe is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies to protect a travelling executive against terrorism, regional spillover, and political repression: advance work on venues and routes, a written threat assessment, an operations controller, and Ministry of Internal Affairs licensed secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a C-suite visit, where the need is layered, documented risk management.

item.title: Executive Protection in Guayaquil
item.description: Executive protection in Guayaquil is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies when it owes a duty of care to an executive facing kidnapping, gang violence, and port-related organised crime: advance work on venues and routes, a written threat assessment, an operations controller, and armed secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a C-suite visit, where the need is layered, documented risk management.

item.title: Executive Protection in La Paz
item.description: Executive protection in La Paz is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies to protect a travelling executive against civil unrest, road blockades, and altitude-related medical risk: advance work on venues and routes, a written risk assessment, an operations controller, and Ministry of Government licensed secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a C-suite visit, where the need is layered, documented risk management.

item.title: Executive Protection in Montevideo
item.description: Executive protection in Montevideo is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies to protect a travelling executive against petty and vehicle crime and periodic demonstration risk: advance work on venues and routes, a written risk assessment, an operations controller, and Ministry of Interior licensed secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a C-suite visit, where the need is layered, documented risk management rather than ad-hoc cover.

item.title: Executive Protection in Ulaanbaatar
item.description: Executive protection in Ulaanbaatar is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies to protect a travelling executive against extreme-weather operational disruption and alcohol-related violence risk: advance work on venues and routes, a written risk assessment, an operations controller, and State Registration Authority approved secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a C-suite visit, where the need is layered, documented risk management.

item.title: Executive Protection in Vientiane
item.description: Executive protection in Vientiane is a programme, not a single officer. It is the discipline a company applies to protect a travelling executive against political-environment and road-safety risk: advance work on venues and routes, a written risk assessment, an operations controller, and Ministry of Public Security registered secure transport integrated with the corporate itinerary. This page is for security managers designing protection around a C-suite visit, where the need is layered, documented risk management rather than ad-hoc cover.

item.title: Hargeisa
item.description: Hargeisa is the capital of Somaliland, a self-declared independent state in the north of what is internationally recognised as Somalia. Somaliland declared independence in 1991 following the collapse of the Somali state, and has since maintained substantially more stability, functional governance, and peace than the rest of Somalia, including the Federal Government’s territory in Mogadishu and the south. Hargeisa is an internationally active city despite Somaliland’s lack of international recognition: international NGOs, development finance organisations, telecoms investors, and the UN all have presence in the city.

item.title: Juba
item.description: Juba is the capital of South Sudan, the world’s youngest nation, which gained independence from Sudan in 2011 and subsequently experienced a devastating civil war from 2013 to 2018. A fragile peace under the Revitalised Agreement (R-ARCSS) has held with periodic interruptions since 2018, but the political and security situation remains fragile, with fighting resuming in some regions in 2024 to 2025. South Sudan is an oil producer, with Petronas, China National Petroleum, and other operators managing production across the country. Juba is also home to one of the world’s largest humanitarian operations, with UNMISS, UNHCR, WFP, MSF, and numerous other organisations managing operations across the country’s fragile post-conflict environment.

item.title: Khartoum
item.description: Khartoum is the capital of Sudan and, as of June 2026, is inaccessible due to the active civil war between the Sudanese Armed Forces (SAF) and the Rapid Support Forces (RSF) that began on 15 April 2023. The conflict has transformed Khartoum into a battleground, causing massive civilian displacement, the destruction of infrastructure including the airport, and the collapse of basic services. More than 10 million Sudanese have been displaced according to UNHCR reporting. The FCDO advises against all travel to Sudan, and the US State Department classifies Sudan at Level 4 (Do Not Travel). Diplomatic missions relocated from Khartoum to Port Sudan in 2023.

item.title: Libreville
item.description: Libreville is the capital and largest city of Gabon, a Central African country with a significant oil sector and a GDP per capita that places it among the upper-middle-income countries in sub-Saharan Africa. The city is the base for most corporate activity in Gabon, including the operations of Perenco, Assala Energy, TotalEnergies, and the manganese mining companies serving the broader national resource sector. International visitors come to Libreville for oil sector business, regulatory engagement, government affairs, and management of resource extraction operations across the country.

item.title: Ndjamena
item.description: N’Djamena is the capital of Chad, a landlocked Sahelian country at the geographic centre of the Lake Chad Basin security complex. Chad borders the territories where Boko Haram and ISWAP operate in the Lake Chad Basin, and N’Djamena has a documented history of terrorist incidents. The political transition following the death of President Idriss Deby in April 2021 and the establishment of a transitional government under Mahamat Idriss Deby has not resolved Chad’s underlying governance and security challenges. Presidential elections were held in 2024. The withdrawal of French Operation Barkhane from N’Djamena in 2023 has changed the regional security architecture in ways that are still unfolding.

item.title: Residential Security in Ashgabat
item.description:
Residential security in Ashgabat addresses the compound protection, staff vetting, and emergency planning requirements of expatriates, NGO personnel, and diplomats based in the city.

The residential environment in Ashgabat

Expatriate residential security in Ashgabat addresses heavy state surveillance, movement restrictions outside Ashgabat, and the regulatory constraints of operating in a highly controlled authoritarian environment and the need for reliable emergency response arrangements. Source: FCDO Turkmenistan travel advice (2026).

What residential security covers in Ashgabat

A residential security programme in Ashgabat covers static compound guarding, vehicle access control, perimeter and CCTV maintenance, domestic staff vetting, and emergency response coordination including medevac planning.

item.title: Residential Security in Asuncion
item.description:
Residential security in Asuncion addresses the compound protection, staff vetting, and emergency planning requirements of expatriates, NGO personnel, and diplomats based in the city.

The residential environment in Asuncion

Expatriate residential security in Asuncion addresses petty crime, bag-snatching, and express kidnapping risk in the informal economy districts and the need for reliable emergency response arrangements. Source: FCDO Paraguay travel advice (2026).

What residential security covers in Asuncion

A residential security programme in Asuncion covers static compound guarding, vehicle access control, perimeter and CCTV maintenance, domestic staff vetting, and emergency response coordination including medevac planning.

item.title: Residential Security in Belize City
item.description: Residential security in Belize City is structured around a high crime rate, limited emergency response capacity, and the additional physical risk of Belize’s hurricane season. The recommended residential zones in Buttonwood Bay and Belama provide the most appropriate base for international personnel, separated from the gang-controlled areas of central Belize City’s Southside district. Generator backup and storm-season preparation are programme essentials rather than optional enhancements. Source: FCDO Belize travel advice (2026); US State Dept Belize Level 2 travel advisory (2026).

item.title: Residential Security in Bishkek
item.description:
Residential security in Bishkek addresses the compound protection, staff vetting, and emergency planning requirements of expatriates, NGO personnel, and diplomats based in the city.

The residential environment in Bishkek

Expatriate residential security in Bishkek addresses petty crime, periodic political unrest, and organised criminal networks with documented involvement in cargo theft and the need for reliable emergency response arrangements. Source: FCDO Kyrgyzstan travel advice (2026).

What residential security covers in Bishkek

A residential security programme in Bishkek covers static compound guarding, vehicle access control, perimeter and CCTV maintenance, domestic staff vetting, and emergency response coordination including medevac planning.

item.title: Residential Security in Chittagong
item.description:
Residential security in Chittagong addresses the compound protection, staff vetting, and emergency planning requirements of expatriates, NGO personnel, and diplomats based in the city.

The residential environment in Chittagong

Expatriate residential security in Chittagong addresses terrorism, political strikes (hartals), and petty crime targeting port and garment-sector visitors and the need for reliable emergency response arrangements. Source: FCDO Bangladesh travel advice (2026).

What residential security covers in Chittagong

A residential security programme in Chittagong covers static compound guarding, vehicle access control, perimeter and CCTV maintenance, domestic staff vetting, and emergency response coordination including medevac planning.

item.title: Residential Security in Dushanbe
item.description:
Residential security in Dushanbe addresses the compound protection, staff vetting, and emergency planning requirements of expatriates, NGO personnel, and diplomats based in the city.

The residential environment in Dushanbe

Expatriate residential security in Dushanbe addresses border proximity risks, political sensitivity, and petty crime in market areas and the need for reliable emergency response arrangements. Source: FCDO Tajikistan travel advice (2026).

What residential security covers in Dushanbe

A residential security programme in Dushanbe covers static compound guarding, vehicle access control, perimeter and CCTV maintenance, domestic staff vetting, and emergency response coordination including medevac planning.

item.title: Residential Security in Georgetown
item.description: Residential security in Georgetown has become a priority concern as Guyana’s oil sector expansion has driven a rapid influx of international personnel and increased the visibility of expatriate residential properties. Georgetown’s crime rate is elevated, with armed robbery and home invasion incidents documented in expatriate-occupied areas. The Guyana Police Force has limited capacity relative to the pace of the city’s growth, and private residential security programmes are the primary mitigation for international staff based in the city. Source: FCDO Guyana travel advice (2026); US State Dept Guyana Level 3 travel advisory (2026).

item.title: Residential Security in Guatemala City
item.description: Residential security in Guatemala City requires structured compound protection, systematic staff vetting, and explicit anti-kidnapping protocols for any international executive or family based in the city. Guatemala City has a high crime rate with kidnapping, extortion, and violent crime affecting multiple zones. The recommended residential zones in Zone 10, Zone 14, and Zone 15 (Vista Hermosa) provide the most appropriate security environment, but structured residential security programming is the required baseline rather than an optional enhancement. Source: FCDO Guatemala travel advice (2026); US State Dept Guatemala Level 3 travel advisory (2026).

item.title: Residential Security in Guayaquil
item.description:
Residential security in Guayaquil addresses the compound protection, staff vetting, and emergency planning requirements of expatriates, NGO personnel, and diplomats based in the city.

The residential environment in Guayaquil

Expatriate residential security in Guayaquil addresses armed robbery, kidnapping for ransom, carjacking, and gang-related violence linked to drug trafficking networks with significant presence in the port and peripheral neighbourhoods and the need for reliable emergency response arrangements. Source: FCDO Ecuador travel advice (2026).

item.title: Residential Security in Kingston
item.description: Residential security in Kingston is a response to a genuinely elevated threat environment. Jamaica’s murder rate of approximately 44 homicides per 100,000 population in 2023 (Jamaica Constabulary Force statistics) is among the highest in the Western Hemisphere, driven by gang activity in defined communities of West and Downtown Kingston. The UK FCDO Jamaica travel advice (2026) advises against all travel to a specific list of Kingston communities. Expatriates and international staff based in Kingston require a structured residential programme that addresses compound security, staff vetting, and emergency response from first arrival.

item.title: Residential Security in La Paz
item.description:
Residential security in La Paz addresses the compound protection, staff vetting, and emergency planning requirements of expatriates, NGO personnel, and diplomats based in the city.

The residential environment in La Paz

Expatriate residential security in La Paz addresses street crime, express kidnapping, political demonstrations that can block major routes, and altitude-related medical risk at 3,640m above sea level and the need for reliable emergency response arrangements. Source: FCDO Bolivia travel advice (2026).

item.title: Residential Security in Managua
item.description: Residential security in Managua addresses both conventional crime risks and the specific challenges created by Nicaragua’s political environment under the Ortega administration. The combination of a conventional high-crime urban environment and the political risk of surveillance, arbitrary detention, and suppression of independent civil society makes residential security planning in Managua more complex than in most Central American capitals. The recommended residential zones in Carretera Masaya, Altamira, and Las Colinas provide the most appropriate base for international staff. Source: FCDO Nicaragua travel advice (2026); US State Dept Nicaragua Level 3 travel advisory (2026).

item.title: Residential Security in Montevideo
item.description:
Residential security in Montevideo addresses the compound protection, staff vetting, and emergency planning requirements of expatriates, NGO personnel, and diplomats based in the city.

The residential environment in Montevideo

Expatriate residential security in Montevideo addresses petty theft in tourist areas and peripheral neighbourhood crime, both at low levels relative to Latin American regional norms and the need for reliable emergency response arrangements. Source: FCDO Uruguay travel advice (2026).

What residential security covers in Montevideo

A residential security programme in Montevideo covers static compound guarding, vehicle access control, perimeter and CCTV maintenance, domestic staff vetting, and emergency response coordination including medevac planning.

item.title: Residential Security in Paramaribo
item.description: Residential security in Paramaribo is calibrated to the city’s medium risk profile: a structured programme addressing property crime, access control, and emergency response planning, without the full-scale compound guarding required in Kingston or Port-au-Prince. Paramaribo is a relatively stable Caribbean capital, but petty crime and opportunistic burglary affect residential properties where perimeter security is absent. The city’s mining and energy sector has brought a growing international population, and residential security programmes for this community are the standard operating baseline. Source: FCDO Suriname travel advice (2026); US State Dept Suriname Level 2 travel advisory (2026).

item.title: Residential Security in Port-au-Prince
item.description: Residential security in Port-au-Prince operates in one of the most challenging urban environments in the Western Hemisphere. Gang alliances control significant portions of the city, including major transit routes, and the national police force operates with severely degraded capacity. The FCDO advises against all travel to Haiti, and the US State Department classifies Haiti at Level 4 (Do Not Travel) as of 2026. Organisations maintaining a presence in Port-au-Prince do so with Haiti-specialist security providers, compound-grade protection in Petion-Ville, and tested evacuation plans. There is no standard residential security programme applicable to Port-au-Prince; every programme is built to the specific compound location and organisational risk tolerance.

item.title: Residential Security in San Salvador
item.description: Residential security in San Salvador is calibrated to a medium risk environment that has improved materially since El Salvador’s large-scale gang crackdown from 2022, but which still warrants professional planning for international executives and their families. The recommended residential zones in Colonia Escalon, Zona Rosa, Santa Elena, and San Benito provide the most appropriate combination of security infrastructure and proximity to business facilities. Source: FCDO El Salvador travel advice (2026); US State Dept El Salvador Level 3 travel advisory (2026).

item.title: Residential Security in Tegucigalpa
item.description: Residential security in Tegucigalpa addresses a persistent high-crime environment that requires structured compound protection, staff vetting, and emergency planning for any international organisation or individual based in the city. Honduras has one of the region’s highest crime rates, with gang activity, extortion, and kidnapping as documented risks affecting expatriate residential communities. The recommended approach is based in the Lomas del Guijarro, Palmira, or Colonia San Carlos zones, with DGSEI-licensed compound guarding and systematic staff background checking. Source: FCDO Honduras travel advice (2026); US State Dept Honduras Level 3 travel advisory (2026).

item.title: Residential Security in Ulaanbaatar
item.description:
Residential security in Ulaanbaatar addresses the compound protection, staff vetting, and emergency planning requirements of expatriates, NGO personnel, and diplomats based in the city.

The residential environment in Ulaanbaatar

Expatriate residential security in Ulaanbaatar addresses petty crime including bag-snatching and pickpocketing, drink-spiking in entertainment districts, and extreme winter conditions and the need for reliable emergency response arrangements. Source: FCDO Mongolia travel advice (2026).

What residential security covers in Ulaanbaatar

A residential security programme in Ulaanbaatar covers static compound guarding, vehicle access control, perimeter and CCTV maintenance, domestic staff vetting, and emergency response coordination including medevac planning.

item.title: Residential Security in Vientiane
item.description:

The residential environment in Vientiane

What residential security covers in Vientiane

A residential security programme in Vientiane covers static compound guarding, vehicle access control, perimeter and CCTV maintenance, domestic staff vetting, and emergency response coordination including medevac planning.

item.title: Residential Security in Yaounde
item.description: Residential security in Yaounde is structured around MINAT-licensed operators, the Bastos diplomatic district as the recommended residential base, and systematic staff vetting for an environment that includes both city-level crime risks and the national context of active armed conflicts in other regions of Cameroon. Oil sector companies, NGOs, and diplomatic missions based in Yaounde all operate residential security programmes in Bastos, and the district’s accumulated security infrastructure makes it the appropriate foundation for any new residential programme. Source: FCDO Cameroon travel advice (2026); US State Dept Cameroon Level 3 travel advisory (2026); MINAT Cameroon.

item.title: Secure Airport Transfers in Belize City
item.description:
Philip S. W. Goldson International Airport is 15km from Belize City’s Fort George hotel district via the Northern Highway. The transfer route approaches the city through areas with moderate crime exposure, and Belize City’s high overall crime level means that vetted, SIA-licensed transport is the professional standard for all business arrivals. Hurricane season (May to November) adds a weather contingency element that is unique to the Belize transfer environment.

The BZE arrival environment

Philip Goldson Airport serves all international flights into Belize, including regional connections from US gateways and Central American hubs. Inside-terminal collection by an SIA-licensed driver eliminates the informal transport exposure at BZE and provides managed transport through the Northern Highway approaches to the city.

item.title: Secure Airport Transfers in Georgetown
item.description: The Cheddi Jagan International Airport to Georgetown transfer is 41km via the East Bank Demerara highway – a single-route, fixed-duration movement that is the most structurally significant security decision on any Georgetown business visit. Georgetown’s elevated robbery and kidnapping risk for oil-sector executives and HNWI, combined with the fixed nature of the transfer route, makes this journey a priority for professional management. Pre-arranged inside-terminal collection by a licensed driver with operations controller oversight eliminates the informal transport and kerb-zone exposure that represents the arrival’s primary risk.

item.title: Secure Airport Transfers in Guatemala City
item.description: La Aurora International Airport is embedded within Guatemala City’s Zone 13, adjacent to Zone 9 – an area with elevated crime and robbery exposure relative to the Zone 10 hotel district where international business visitors are based. The 7km transfer between GUA and Zone 10 therefore traverses a meaningful security zone change, not a uniform safe corridor. Inside-terminal collection by a Gobernacion UESSP-licensed driver with operations controller management is the required approach for professional arrivals at La Aurora.

item.title: Secure Airport Transfers in Kingston
item.description: Norman Manley International Airport sits on the Palisadoes causeway peninsula, and the 20km transfer to New Kingston is the first security decision of any Kingston business visit. The transfer route traverses sections of Kingston with elevated crime exposure – and informal transport from KIN is a documented risk for arriving international visitors unfamiliar with the city. Pre-arranged PSRA-licensed collection inside the arrivals hall eliminates informal transport exposure and provides operations controller managed movement from landing to hotel arrival.

item.title: Secure Airport Transfers in Managua
item.description: Augusto C. Sandino International Airport is 12km from the Carretera Masaya hotel corridor via the Carretera Norte – a transfer that traverses urban Managua approaches carrying higher crime exposure than the business district itself. Inside-terminal collection by a DGPN-licensed driver with operations controller management is the standard approach for professional arrivals in Managua. Nicaragua’s political surveillance environment adds a further reason to use pre-arranged, documented vetted transport rather than informal options for all international business visitors.

item.title: Secure Airport Transfers in Paramaribo
item.description: The Johan Adolf Pengel International Airport to Paramaribo transfer is 45km via the South Paramaribo highway – a transit of 45 to 60 minutes under standard conditions and significantly longer during the rainy seasons when flooding affects the route. This distance, combined with the limited transport infrastructure serving the airport, makes the Pengel transfer the most significant logistics and security planning point on any Suriname business visit. Pre-arranged inside-terminal collection by a Ministry of Justice-licensed driver eliminates informal transport exposure and provides operations controller oversight for the entire highway transfer.

item.title: Secure Airport Transfers in Port-au-Prince
item.description:
The Toussaint Louverture International Airport transfer in Port-au-Prince is one of the highest-risk airport transfers in the world in the current security environment. Gang activity in the lower city and on the airport approach corridors has made the PAP transfer a planning centrepiece for any Port-au-Prince engagement. Inside-terminal collection with armed escort, real-time gang-route intelligence, and flexible route selection are baseline requirements – not optional security enhancements.

The PAP arrival environment

Toussaint Louverture International Airport is located in the lower city, in the zone most directly affected by gang activity. The route to Petionville – where the Hotel Montana, Villa Creole, and international organisation offices are located – passes through areas where gang control has been documented and where kidnapping targeting arriving international visitors has occurred. This makes the PAP transfer fundamentally different from most airport security planning.

item.title: Secure Airport Transfers in San Salvador
item.description: Monsenor Oscar Arnulfo Romero International Airport is 44km from San Salvador’s Escalon and Zona Rosa business district via the Comalapa highway. The 44km distance makes the SAL transfer the most significant logistics element of any El Salvador business visit. El Salvador’s post-2022 security improvements have substantially reduced the crime risk on the Comalapa corridor compared with previous years, but pre-arranged PNC-registered transfer with inside-terminal collection and operations controller management remains the professional standard for corporate arrivals.

item.title: Secure Airport Transfers in Tegucigalpa
item.description: Toncontin International Airport is one of the Americas’ most unusual airport environments – an international airport embedded in Tegucigalpa’s urban fabric, approached by a challenging mountain descent and surrounded by mixed residential and commercial areas with elevated crime exposure. The transfer from TGU to the Lomas del Guijarro hotel district, though short in road distance, passes through urban Tegucigalpa rather than the controlled environment of most international airports. Inside-terminal collection by a DGSEI-licensed driver with operations controller management is the required approach for all professional arrivals.

item.title: Secure Airport Transfers in Yaounde
item.description:
Yaounde Nsimalen International Airport is 25km from the Bastos diplomatic district and the Hilton Yaounde via the main Yaounde highway approach. The 25km transfer traverses peri-urban and mixed residential areas with moderate-to-elevated robbery risk – making inside-terminal collection by a MINAT-licensed driver with operations controller management the required approach for professional arrivals into the Cameroonian capital.

The NSI arrival environment

Yaounde Nsimalen Airport serves all international flights into Yaounde, including Air France connections from Paris and regional hub services via Addis Ababa and Nairobi. The 25km highway transfer is the most significant security element of any Yaounde arrival and requires pre-arranged management.

item.title: Security Drivers in Ashgabat
item.description:
Security drivers in Ashgabat are licensed under Turkmenistan’s relevant regulatory framework, providing vetted transport for airport transfers and in-city movement with full operations controller support.

The Ashgabat transport security environment

A vetted security driver provides significantly better risk management than informal transport for visitors to Ashgabat. The primary concerns are heavy state surveillance, movement restrictions outside Ashgabat, and the regulatory constraints of operating in a highly controlled authoritarian environment. Source: FCDO Turkmenistan travel advice (2026).

What security drivers cover in Asuncion

Security driver operations in Asuncion cover airport transfers with inside-terminal collection at Silvio Pettirossi International Airport (ASU), in-city movement with pre-planned primary and secondary routes, vehicle discipline protocol throughout all journeys, and operations controller tracking from departure to confirmed arrival.

item.title: Ashgabat
item.description: Ashgabat is the capital of Turkmenistan, one of the world’s most closed and controlled states. Built from natural gas revenues into a city of white marble government buildings and monumental architecture, Ashgabat presents a unique operating environment for international business visitors: the physical security risk is low, but the surveillance environment, visa restrictions, photography prohibitions, and government monitoring of foreign nationals create operational parameters that differ fundamentally from most business capitals. International business visits are driven almost entirely by Turkmenistan’s massive natural gas sector.

item.title: Asuncion
item.description: Asuncion is the capital of Paraguay, a landlocked South American republic whose economy is driven by agriculture (soy and beef exports), hydropower, and a significant parallel economy associated with its Triple Frontier border with Brazil and Argentina. The city has a moderate security environment for business visitors, with the primary risks being armed robbery in certain areas and after dark, the presence of organised crime networks linked to regional narcotics and contraband trade, and a corruption environment that requires careful counterparty due diligence for government-facing commercial activity.

item.title: Belize City
item.description: Belize City is the largest city and commercial capital of Belize, with a population of approximately 80,000 in the city proper. Although Belmopan became the official capital after Hurricane Hattie devastated Belize City in 1961, virtually all international business activity, the main port, the primary international airport, and the majority of diplomatic missions remain in Belize City. The city serves as the gateway for Belize’s tourism industry, anchored by cruise ship arrivals via Tourism Village, as well as the administrative base for the country’s offshore financial services sector, logistics and port operations, and oil and agricultural industries. Belize’s English-speaking, common law legal system and Caribbean location make it an accessible entry point for Central American and Caribbean business for many international investors.

item.title: Bishkek
item.description: Bishkek is the capital of Kyrgyzstan, a landlocked Central Asian republic wedged between Kazakhstan, Uzbekistan, Tajikistan, and China. The city is the administrative and commercial centre of a country whose economy is driven by gold mining, remittances from labour migrants in Russia and Kazakhstan, and a growing services sector. Bishkek is the most politically volatile capital in Central Asia by recent history: three governments have been removed by popular uprisings since 2005, and the political environment retains an unpredictability that distinguishes it from the more controlled authoritarian systems of its neighbours.

item.title: Bodyguard Hire in Kabul
item.description:
Afghanistan’s post-2021 environment means routine bodyguard hire, in the conventional sense, is not available in Kabul; the FCDO and US State Department advise against all travel. This page covers what engagement actually requires: Taliban-approved local partners, IS-KP threat awareness, and acceptance that no Western consular backstop exists. Any access is planned individually around current authorisation pathways rather than arranged as a standard, on-demand service.

The Kabul Security Environment After 2021

The August 2021 transition removed the Western-backed government and its security apparatus and replaced it with Taliban governance. For security operations, this means one thing above all others: Taliban approval is not a bureaucratic formality but an operational prerequisite. Security companies that operated under the previous government cannot simply continue under the same model. Operators who have not rebuilt their relationships and authorisation pathways within the post-2021 framework cannot function reliably in Kabul, regardless of their pre-2021 credentials.

item.description: Port-au-Prince requires the clearest possible statement of the risk before discussing hire: gang networks control approximately 85 percent of the metropolitan area, and both FCDO and the US State Department advise against all travel. Armed security personnel require specific authorisation, so hire pairs a licensed officer with secure transport, scaled to the itinerary rather than a fixed contract.

item.title: Bodyguard Hire in San Salvador
item.description: Hiring a bodyguard in San Salvador is shaped by a residual gang environment and state surveillance and monitoring rather than the acute gang violence of prior years, plus residual extortion risk. This page covers how close protection hire works in El Salvador, what a Ministerio de Justicia y Seguridad Publica registered officer does across a day of movements, and how quickly cover is arranged. Hire pairs a registered officer with secure transport, scaled to the itinerary rather than a fixed contract.

item.title: Bodyguard Hire in Santiago
item.description: Hiring a bodyguard in Santiago is usually tied to a specific visit, where civil unrest and vehicle targeting shape the plan more than everyday violent crime. This page covers how close protection hire works in Chile, what an RNGDS-registered officer does across a day of movements, and how quickly cover is arranged. Armed cover is practiced for higher-risk profiles including mining and political principals, so hire pairs a registered officer with secure transport, scaled to the itinerary.

item.title: Bodyguard Hire in Tegucigalpa
item.description: Hiring a bodyguard in Tegucigalpa is usually tied to a specific visit, where gang violence, organised crime, and kidnapping and extortion shape the plan. This page covers how close protection hire works in Honduras, what a Secretaria de Seguridad authorised officer does across a day of movements, and how quickly cover is arranged. Armed cover requires specific state authorisation, so hire pairs an authorised officer with secure transport, scaled to the itinerary rather than a fixed contract.

item.title: Bodyguard Hire in Ulaanbaatar
item.description: Hiring a bodyguard in Ulaanbaatar is typically about extreme weather disruption and alcohol-related violence rather than violent crime targeting foreigners specifically. This page covers how close protection hire works in Mongolia, what a State Registration Authority approved operator does across a day of movements, and how quickly cover is arranged. Most operations use unarmed personnel, so hire pairs a licensed operator with secure transport, scaled to the itinerary rather than a fixed contract.

item.title: Bodyguard Hire in Vientiane
item.description: Hiring a bodyguard in Vientiane is typically about the political environment and road safety rather than violent crime, which is low. This page covers how close protection hire works in Laos, what a Ministry of Public Security registered operator provides across a day of movements, and how quickly cover is arranged. Armed private security is not the standard commercial model, so hire pairs an unarmed registered operator with secure transport, scaled to the itinerary rather than a fixed contract.

item.title: Bodyguard Hire in Yaounde
item.description: Hiring a bodyguard in Yaounde is usually tied to a specific visit, where regional terrorism and the Anglophone conflict spillover shape the plan alongside political demonstrations. This page covers how close protection hire works in Cameroon, what a MINAT-authorised operator does across a day of movements, and how quickly cover is arranged. Armed cover requires specific MINAT authorisation, so hire pairs an authorised officer with secure transport, scaled to the itinerary rather than a fixed contract.

item.title: Chittagong
item.description: Chittagong (officially Chattogram) is Bangladesh’s second-largest city and the country’s principal seaport, handling approximately 90% of Bangladesh’s import-export trade. It is the operational hub for the ready-made garment industry’s export infrastructure, Bangladesh’s petroleum refining sector, and a significant shipbuilding and ship-breaking industry. International business travel to Chittagong is driven primarily by the garment sector – international buyers, compliance auditors, and brand due diligence teams visiting manufacturing operations – alongside shipping, energy, and infrastructure sector visitors.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Caracas
item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Caracas: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Caracas network holds current SENAS registration, passes documented background checks, and is assessed for Venezuela-specific competence given institutional pressures on verification. It covers standard team configuration, extra due-diligence steps, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Douala
item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Douala: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Douala network holds MINAT authorisation, with registration and identification documentation, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration, armed authorisation for high-risk principals, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Erbil
item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Erbil: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Erbil network holds a licence from the Kurdistan Regional Government Ministry of Interior, separate from Baghdad’s federal framework, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration, the armed-as-standard model for senior principals, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Jeddah
item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Jeddah: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Jeddah network holds GASC licensing from the Ministry of Interior, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration, armed authorisation for high-risk contexts including industrial sites, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Kabul
item.description:
This page is about who can realistically support an Afghanistan-linked security requirement under current conditions: there is no functioning civil private security licensing framework, and any Afghan security company still operating does so under Taliban arrangements. It covers how those relationships are vetted, why conventional CPO deployment is not offered without rebuilt post-2021 authorisation, and the extreme due-diligence standard applied to any specialist partner we work with.

The operating model: compound-based, Afghan-led

Western CPO firms largely withdrew from Afghanistan after the 2021 evacuation. Effective CPO operations in Kabul depend entirely on vetted Afghan operators with post-2021 operational experience, Taliban relationship management capability, and proven performance in the IS-KP threat environment. The compound-based operating model minimises external exposure: movements outside secured compounds are treated as high-risk events, planned individually, and conducted with IS-KP target pattern intelligence informing every routing decision.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Kano
item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Kano: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Kano network holds an NSCDC Kano State Command licence, with verifiable licence numbers, and is assessed for local competence given the elevated terrorism and intercommunal-violence risk. It covers standard team configuration and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Karachi
item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Karachi: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Karachi network is licensed under the Sindh Private Security Companies Ordinance, holds a valid arms licence where armed, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration, security clearance requirements, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Kathmandu: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Kathmandu network is registered under Nepal’s 2017 Security Personnel and Private Security Organizations Act, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration, police coordination requirements for armed cover, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Kuwait City: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Kuwait City network holds Ministry of Interior licensing, with appropriate arms authorisation where armed, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration, the licensing that sets Kuwait apart from some Gulf neighbours, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

Every operator in our Lahore network holds a licence from Punjab’s Home Department under the Security Companies (Regulation) Act, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration, the widely available armed option, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

Every operator in our Manama network holds a licence from the Ministry of Interior’s General Directorate of Criminal Investigation and Forensic Science, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration, the not-routinely-armed model, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

Every operator in our Panama City network holds a licence from the Directorate of Private Security Services, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration, individual personnel certification, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

It covers standard team configuration and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

Every operator in our Port Harcourt network holds an NSCDC licence, publicly verifiable via the NSCDC register, with individual armed-guard registration, and is assessed for oil-sector-specific competence. It covers standard team configuration and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

It covers standard team configuration, widely available armed cover, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

Every operator in our San Jose network holds Ministry of Security authorisation, renewed annually, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration, individual officer certification requirements, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

Every operator in our Santiago network is registered with RNGDS, passes documented background checks and training certification, and is assessed for Chile-specific competence. It covers standard team configuration, armed cover for mining-sector and political principals, and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

item.title: Close Protection Officers in Santo Domingo
item.description: This page is about the people who provide close protection in Santo Domingo: who they are, how they are vetted, and what credentials to verify before they deploy. Every operator in our Santo Domingo network is licensed by the Ministry of Interior and Police, with separate registration for armed personnel, and is assessed for local competence. It covers standard team configuration and the credential checks a buyer should insist on before engaging any officer.

item.title: Dushanbe
item.description: Dushanbe is the capital of Tajikistan, the poorest and most mountainous of the five former Soviet Central Asian republics. It is the administrative hub for a country that shares a long and internationally significant border with Afghanistan, and whose economy is dominated by aluminium production, hydropower, and remittances. International business travel to Dushanbe is driven primarily by energy sector projects – the CASA-1000 power transmission project, the Roghun hydropower dam, and regional grid connectivity – alongside development finance organisations active in one of the world’s most heavily aid-dependent economies.

item.title: Event Security in Caracas
item.description: Caracas is one of the most complex event security environments in Latin America. FCDO advises against all but essential travel to Venezuela, and the combination of kidnapping, colectivo territorial presence, state surveillance (SEBIN), and the logistical complications of Venezuela’s USD cash economy makes event security planning here materially different from other regional locations. Events do take place: the eastern safe zones of Altamira, Las Mercedes, and Chacao retain functional hotel and conference infrastructure. The security architecture required to run them well is substantial.

item.title: Event Security in Douala
item.description: Douala is Cameroon’s economic capital and the country’s largest city, housing the main seaport, the headquarters of most international companies, and the operational base for the energy, agri-business, and infrastructure sectors. It hosts corporate conferences, investor briefings, and operational meetings for multinationals active in the Central African region. Event security in Douala addresses a consistent armed robbery and vehicle crime environment, the political spillover from Cameroon’s Anglophone armed conflict, and after-dark risks that require specific transport protocols for any evening event programme.

item.title: Event Security in Erbil
item.description: Erbil is the capital of the Kurdistan Region of Iraq and the operational hub for the KRI’s substantial oil and gas sector. It hosts BP, TotalEnergies, DNO, Genel Energy, and Gulf Keystone Petroleum, alongside a significant KRG government, diplomatic, and NGO presence. Events in Erbil are substantially more frequent than their security risk context suggests to outside observers: the city functions as a working business hub, not an emergency-only location. The security architecture required for Erbil events reflects a genuinely elevated threat profile, driven by documented rocket and drone attacks by Iran-aligned militia groups, residual ISIL insurgent capability in border areas, and the unpredictable KRG-Baghdad political dynamic.

item.title: Event Security in Jeddah
item.description: Jeddah is Saudi Arabia’s commercial capital and the primary gateway for international business associated with Vision 2030 investment projects, energy sector operations, and the Kingdom’s expanding entertainment and hospitality economy. The city hosts major conferences in the Vision 2030 framework, events associated with the Formula E circuit, and private gatherings of senior Saudi and international business principals. Event security in Jeddah operates against a backdrop of two specific risk factors that have no equivalent in most other major conference cities: the cross-border Houthi missile and drone threat, and Saudi Arabia’s strict legal code governing conduct, photography, and alcohol.

item.title: Event Security in Kabul
item.description: Kabul sits at the extreme end of the event security risk spectrum. FCDO advises against all travel to Afghanistan, and the IS-KP terrorism threat since the August 2021 Taliban takeover has created an environment where mass-casualty attacks at gatherings remain an active and documented threat. The 2021 HKIA bombing, killing approximately 170 people, and subsequent attacks on Kabul mosques, checkpoints, and international-presence locations, illustrate consistent IS-KP capability and intent. This context defines every element of event security planning for Kabul.

item.title: Event Security in Kano
item.description: Kano is northern Nigeria’s largest city and commercial capital, with a significant trading, manufacturing, and agricultural commodity economy. It hosts a range of development sector, UN agency, and corporate events, particularly from organisations operating in Nigeria’s north. Event security in Kano requires the highest level of threat awareness of any regular Nigerian business hub: the FCDO advises against all but essential travel to Kano State due to the terrorism threat from Boko Haram and ISWAP, intercommunal violence has produced documented episodes of city-wide movement restriction, and the general crime environment applies a consistent risk burden on foreign national attendees.

item.title: Event Security in Karachi
item.description: Karachi is Pakistan’s commercial capital and the country’s principal hub for multinational corporate, energy sector, and NGO activity. FCDO notes the risk of terrorism, civil unrest, and criminality across Pakistan and advises against all but essential travel to certain parts of Karachi. The city’s event security environment is shaped by TTP-linked activity, Baloch separatist operations, sectarian violence, kidnap-for-ransom targeting of corporate individuals, and the bandh (general strike) risk that can shut all transport city-wide at short notice. With Sindh-licensed operators, appropriate venue selection, and a bandh contingency protocol, major corporate events are operationally viable in the Clifton, DHA, and Bath Island districts.

item.title: Event Security in Kathmandu
item.description: Kathmandu is Nepal’s capital and a significant destination for development sector conferences, UN-affiliated meetings, trekking and adventure tourism industry events, and multinational corporate gatherings with Nepal or broader South Asia scope. The security environment is elevated rather than critical: the primary planning variables are political bandhs that can ground transport at short notice, the residual seismic risk affecting building stock across Kathmandu Valley since the 2015 earthquake, and petty crime in the main tourist districts. Nepal’s Security Personnel and Private Security Organizations Act 2017 provides a well-structured regulatory framework for the private security sector, and registered Nepali operators provide a capable and compliant security layer for corporate events.

item.title: Event Security in Kuwait City
item.description:
Kuwait City sits at a strategic intersection of Gulf commerce and regional geopolitical tension. Hosting a corporate conference, product launch, or private function in the capital requires security planning that accounts for both the elevated national threat picture and the specific operational requirements of the venue and guest profile.

Threat Context for Event Planners

Kuwait’s security environment is shaped by proximity to Iran-aligned militia networks that have historically targeted US-affiliated installations. Camp Arifjan and Camp Buehring have been referenced in Kataib Hezbollah communications, and disrupted plots in 2020 and 2024 underline that domestic security services remain actively engaged. Events involving US government-linked organisations, American multinationals, or high-profile Western executives warrant explicit threat assessment as part of the pre-event planning phase.

item.title: Event Security in Lahore
item.description: Lahore is Pakistan’s cultural and economic capital, the seat of Punjab province, and one of South Asia’s largest cities. It attracts corporate events from the textile, banking, and infrastructure sectors, as well as diplomatic conferences and investment briefings. Event security in Lahore operates against a formally assessed very-high terrorism threat, a documented kidnapping risk to foreign nationals, and a political environment that can shift rapidly in the period around demonstrations and political confrontations.

item.title: Event Security in Manama
item.description:
Manama is the financial capital of Bahrain and the Gulf Cooperation Council’s primary hub for Islamic finance conferences, investment forums, and sovereign-level diplomatic events. The Bahrain International Exhibition and Convention Centre, the waterfront venues in Bahrain Bay, and the Formula 1 hospitality infrastructure all generate events requiring professional security planning calibrated to the specific Gulf threat environment.

The event security environment in Manama

The FCDO advises normal travel precautions for Bahrain while noting that the threat from terrorism across the wider Middle East and Gulf region remains high. The political environment carries residual sensitivity from the 2011 Arab Spring period, with demonstrations historically occurring in outer townships – Sitra, Bilad Al Qadeem, Sanabis – but not affecting the central business district or primary conference venues.
